title: İskender kebabı
subtitle: Lamb over flatbread with tomato sauce, yoghurt, and sizzling butter
excerpt: Thin-sliced spiced lamb over torn flatbread, bathed in a simple tomato sauce, topped with cold plain yoghurt, and finished with clarified butter poured over sizzling at the table. A dish from Bursa that depends on the contrast between warm, saucy lamb and cold, sharp yoghurt.
sourceNotes: İskender kebabı is named after İskender Efendi, a kebab-maker in Bursa who is credited with its invention in the mid-nineteenth century. It is a protected dish associated with the city of Bursa in northwestern Turkey. The original uses thinly sliced döner lamb; this version adapts the preparation for home cooking using spiced lamb mince formed into thin patties and sliced. The defining components (flatbread base, tomato sauce, cold yoghurt, and hot butter) are consistent across all versions.

content.text: The genius of iskender is the contrast of temperatures. The flatbread soaks up warm tomato sauce and becomes silky and soft at the edges while staying slightly chewy in the middle. The lamb goes over the top, hot from the grill. Then comes the yoghurt, cold and sharp, spooned over. And finally the butter, clarified and heated until golden, poured sizzling directly over the yoghurt so it melts into the pool. You eat it all together, each component distinct, none of them quite matching in temperature.

The lamb should be thin and slightly charred. In Turkey it comes from the rotating döner spit, sliced to order. At home, the closest approximation is lamb mince formed into thin, flat patties, cooked in a very hot dry pan until charred on both sides, and then sliced. The ratio of butter to yoghurt matters: the butter amount should feel excessive. It is not.

items.name: Lamb mince
items.prepNote: seasoned with 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp paprika, salt, and black pepper

items.name: Sweet paprika
items.prepNote: smoked or sweet, for the butter

content.text: Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes and season with salt. Simmer for 12 minutes until the sauce is thick and concentrated. Keep warm.

Season the {{lamb-mince}} with cumin, paprika, salt, and pepper. Mix well. Shape into thin, flat patties about 1 cm thick. Heat a dry frying pan over high heat until very hot. Cook the patties for 2 to 3 minutes per side until charred and cooked through. Slice into thin strips.

Divide the torn {{pitta-bread}} among four warm plates. Spoon the tomato sauce generously over the bread to soak it. Lay the sliced lamb over the bread. Spoon the cold {{plain-yoghurt}} over the lamb. In a small pan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium-high heat until it foams and turns golden. Add the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, swirl, and immediately pour the sizzling butter over each plate at the table. Eat immediately.

items.fix: Assemble just before serving; the sauce should soak the top surface of the bread, not waterlog it
items.cause: Too much tomato sauce, or bread soaked for too long before eating
items.symptom: Flatbread is soggy throughout rather than saucy on top

items.fix: Rest the patties for 3 minutes after cooking; use mince with at least 15% fat
items.cause: Mince too lean, or not rested before slicing
items.symptom: Lamb patties fall apart when sliced

items.fix: Watch the butter constantly; it goes from golden to burned in seconds; pour the moment it turns pale brown
items.cause: Heat too high, or butter cooked for too long
items.symptom: Butter burned rather than turning golden

items.fix: Assemble just before serving. The sauce should soak the top of the bread, not turn it to mush.
items.cause: Yoghurt not cold, or lamb not hot enough
items.symptom: Dish tastes flat without the contrast of temperatures

content.text: İskender is one of the few Turkish dishes with a known creator and date. A kebab-maker named İskender Efendi in Bursa, in the mid-1800s, served döner lamb over flatbread with tomato sauce for the first time. The family that carries his name still runs restaurants in Bursa. Outside Turkey the dish is well known in Turkish neighbourhoods across Europe. The home version with minced lamb keeps the key parts: the sauced bread, the hot meat, the cold yoghurt, and the sizzling butter.
